records.summary: Japan-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2014 after an insolvency-related collapse.
records.notes: Official dates split: civil rehabilitation application filed 2014-02-28; bankruptcy proceedings commenced 2014-04-24.

records.summary: Bahamas-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2022 after an insolvency-related collapse.
records.notes: Archived access is preferred because the original domain no longer serves as the primary public destination. Official case materials reflect the 2022-11-11 petition date.

records.summary: Centralized exchange launched in 2017 and still operating under the Binance brand.
records.notes: Official Binance announcement set the exchange launch for 2017-07-14.

records.summary: Defunct Canadian centralized cryptocurrency exchange that entered creditor protection in February 2019 after founder Gerald Cotten's death and missing-customer-funds crisis, later moving into bankruptcy. HEI records QuadrigaCX as a dead event-backed exchange with insolvency and regulatory-review history.
records.notes: Platform shut down 2019-01-28; CCAA protection granted 2019-02-05.

records.summary: Defunct cryptocurrency exchange founded in 2011 and taken offline in July 2017 after U.S. law-enforcement actions and the arrest of Alexander Vinnik. HEI records BTC-e as a dead event-backed exchange due to the 2017 indictment, FinCEN penalty, domain/service disruption, and later guilty plea by Vinnik.
records.notes: The stored record uses the 2017-07-26 FinCEN enforcement date as the operational end marker.

records.summary: Ethereum-born decentralized exchange protocol family first deployed to mainnet in 2018 and still active through multiple major protocol versions under the Uniswap brand.
records.notes: Entity-level umbrella record. v0/v1, v2, and v3 milestones are recorded here. Deployment-level and chain-level modeling is still pending, so this is not yet split by deployment.

records.summary: Ethereum-born decentralized exchange and liquidity-aggregation protocol family launched in August 2020 and still active as a multi-chain DeFi stack under the Sushi brand.
records.notes: Entity-level umbrella record. Launch is kept at month precision because official Sushi materials describe the project as created in late August 2020. Deployment-level and product-level modeling are still pending.

records.summary: New Zealand-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2019 after a hack-related crisis.
records.notes: Official sources confirm a January 2019 hack and liquidation in May 2019. The stored record uses 2019-05-14 as the operational end marker based on the liquidator appointment date.

records.summary: United States-based centralized exchange launched in 2011 and still operating under the Kraken brand.
records.notes: Launch date updated from Kraken’s official founding post dated 2011-07-28.

records.summary: Luxembourg-based centralized exchange launched in 2011 and still operating under the Bitstamp brand.
UAE-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the Bybit brand.
Major U.S.-based centralized cryptocurrency exchange. HEI records Coinbase as an active exchange with event-backed history, including a 2023 SEC civil action and the 2025 dismissal of that action.
Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the OKX brand.
records.notes: Current OKX materials describe the exchange as founded in 2017, while this record preserves earlier lineage to the Okcoin/OKEx ecosystem in the stored launch date.

records.summary: Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2017 and still operating under the KuCoin brand.
Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the MEXC brand.
records.notes: Official MEXC materials state that the exchange has operated since 2018.

records.summary: Cayman Islands-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the Gate.io brand.

Major centralized cryptocurrency exchange founded in 2012 and operated by iFinex. HEI records Bitfinex as an active exchange with event-backed history, including a 2016 CFTC order, the 2016 Bitfinex hack, and the 2022 U.S. seizure of bitcoin connected to the hack.
records.notes: Available evidence supports the recorded Bitfinex launch date; the date is retained from the best available official anniversary material.

records.summary: United States-based centralized exchange launched in 2015 and still operating under the Gemini brand.
records.notes: Gemini company materials describe the business as founded in 2014, while the exchange officially opened trading on 2015-10-08.

records.summary: Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the HTX brand.
Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2019 and still operating under the Crypto.com Exchange brand.

South Korean centralized cryptocurrency exchange. In February 2026 it accidentally credited users with approximately 620,000 BTC in an internal promotional payout / ledger error, temporarily restricted affected accounts, recovered most erroneous balances, and came under regulatory inspection. The exchange remained active.
records.notes: Do not mark dead or hacked. The February 2026 incident was reported as an internal ledger / promotional payout error rather than an external theft. Track it as a severe operational incident with affected-account restrictions and regulatory review while keeping the entity active.

records.summary: Japan-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the bitFlyer brand.
records.notes: Available evidence supports the recorded bitFlyer founding date;the stored date follows the best available available source.

records.summary: Japan-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Coincheck brand.

South Korean centralized exchange operated by Dunamu. In November 2025 it suffered a Solana hot-wallet / abnormal withdrawal incident involving roughly USD 30-40 million in assets, suspended deposits and withdrawals, and later resumed Solana-related services in phases.
records.notes: Do not mark dead. The 2025 Solana incident was severe, but reporting and exchange notices describe reimbursement, suspended transfers, and staged resumption rather than terminal closure.

records.summary: Professional trading surface within the broader Kraken exchange ecosystem.
records.notes: Launch date updated from Kraken’s official announcement introducing Kraken Pro on 2022-12-22.

records.summary: Gibraltar-based centralized exchange launched in 2021 and still operating under the Bullish brand.
British Virgin Islands-based centralized exchange launched in 2015 and still operating under the LBank brand.
Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the Bitget brand.
Japan-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Bitbank brand.
United Kingdom-based centralized exchange that remains online in a limited or transitional operating state.
records.notes: Official site showed a migration/update notice in April 2026 with customer assets stated to remain safe.

records.summary: Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2023 after a voluntary shutdown.
records.notes: Regulatory pressure forms part of the documented shutdown context.

records.summary: Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2023 after a voluntary shutdown.
records.notes: Deteriorating operating conditions and investigation-related disruption formed part of the shutdown context.

records.summary: United States-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Poloniex brand.
records.notes: Poloniex acquisition and later Circle spinout are modeled as ownership events rather than terminal death-side status.

records.summary: Brazil-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the Mercado Bitcoin brand.
South Korea-based centralized exchange launched in October 2014 and still operating under the Coinone brand.
records.notes: Launch timing corrected from 2015 to October 2014 based on Coinone official materials.

records.summary: Mexico-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Bitso brand.
Thailand-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the Bitkub brand.
Brazil-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Foxbit brand.
Australia-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the BTC Markets brand.
Turkey-based centralized exchange launched in 2017 and still operating under the Paribu brand.
Indonesia-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Indodax brand.
United Kingdom-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the Luno brand.
Philippines-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Coins.ph brand.
Ukraine-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the WhiteBIT brand.

Centralized exchange that suffered a hot-wallet security incident in January 2025, temporarily suspended deposits and withdrawals, and later restored withdrawal services. It is modeled as active with major incident events rather than a death-side record.
records.notes: Official Phemex timeline is the primary source for the incident, suspension, staged restoration, and final February 2025 withdrawal restoration.

records.summary: Netherlands-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the Bitvavo brand.
Indonesia-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the Tokocrypto brand.
South Korea-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the Korbit brand.
Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the Bitrue brand.
Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the XT.COM brand.

Indian centralized cryptocurrency exchange that confirmed a July 2025 security breach involving an internal operational account, with reports placing the loss at about USD 44 million. CoinDCX said customer funds were not affected and later restored normal withdrawal/service operations.
records.notes: Do not mark dead. The July 2025 incident involved an internal operational account according to company statements and reporting, not customer-custody loss. Track hack/security incident and restoration events while keeping status active.

records.summary: United Arab Emirates-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the BTSE brand.
Australia-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the Independent Reserve brand.
Chile-based centralized exchange launched in 2015 and still operating under the Buda.com brand.
Argentina-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the Ripio brand.
Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Coinhako brand.
Philippines-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the PDAX brand.
South Africa-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the VALR brand.
Taiwan-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the BitoPro brand.
Spain-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Bit2Me brand.
India-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the ZebPay brand.

Cryptocurrency derivatives exchange founded in 2014. HEI records BitMEX as an active exchange with event-backed regulatory history, including U.S. enforcement actions, a 2021 CFTC/FinCEN settlement, founder guilty pleas, and a later company plea/fine.
Japan-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Zaif brand.
United Arab Emirates-based centralized exchange launched in 2017 and still operating under the CoinW brand.
Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2021 and still operating under the OrangeX brand.
Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the AscendEX brand.
Cayman Islands-based centralized exchange launched in 2022 and still operating under the Toobit brand.

Crypto exchange candidate from the CCXT-supported exchange list and official Backpack Exchange domain. HEI records it as an active centralized exchange seed because it has an official trading-domain identity beyond a single database row.
Australia-based centralized exchange launched in 2013 and still operating under the CoinJar brand.
Australia-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the Swyftx brand.
Bahrain-based centralized exchange launched in 2017 and still operating under the Rain brand.
Mexico-based centralized exchange launched in 2020 and still operating under the Trubit brand.

Iranian centralized cryptocurrency exchange. In June 2025 it was hit by a politically motivated hack attributed to Predatory Sparrow / Gonjeshke Darande, with roughly USD 90 million in crypto burned or destroyed and the website/app taken offline during incident review.
records.notes: Do not mark dead yet. Reuters and other sources report a major hack and offline website/app state, but current evidence does not prove permanent shutdown, liquidation, or service death. Keep as limited unless a later closure or durable recovery marker is confirmed.

Centralized exchange launched in 2021 and still operating under the Bitunix brand.

Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2022 and still operating under the Aibit brand.
Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2023 and still operating under the Ourbit brand.
Switzerland-based centralized exchange launched in 2015 and still operating under the Lykke Exchange brand.

Fastex ecosystem centralized exchange that entered a withdrawal-only / service-termination process and whose official-domain pages stated that exchange operations would halt on April 1, 2026 at 23:59 Yerevan time.
records.notes: Classified as dead / regulation rather than limited because official Fastex-domain pages displayed a termination notice for exchange operations. MiCA / regulatory alignment is reported as the driver by secondary coverage, while official-domain pages are used for the terminal operations marker. Confidence remains medium because the reason is supported indirectly.

records.summary: Seychelles-based centralized exchange launched in 2017 and still operating under the ChangeNOW Pro brand.
Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2018 and still operating under the BingX brand.
Chile-based centralized exchange launched in 2016 and still operating under the CryptoMarket brand.
Singapore-based centralized exchange launched in 2021 and still operating under the Coinstore brand.
Centralized exchange launched in 2021 and still operating under the BVOX brand.
Anonymous cryptocurrency exchange that was dismantled by the RCMP on 2025-09-18 after investigators said it contravened Canadian AML rules and client-identification requirements.
records.notes: Updated from external listing request. Death reason is set to regulation because the terminal event is a documented RCMP seizure and dismantling tied to Canadian compliance failures.

records.summary: Ukraine-based centralized exchange launched in 2019 and still operating under the TimeX brand.
Switzerland-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Bity brand.
Poland-based centralized exchange launched in 2014 and still operating under the Paybis Exchange brand.
United States-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2016 after an insolvency-related collapse.
records.notes: Later reporting places the exchange launch in May 2013. The operational end marker uses the January 2016 disclosure of hidden losses and withdrawal failure.

records.summary: Italy-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2018 after a hack-related crisis.
records.notes: Founding year is treated as 2017 from retrospective reporting. Death marker uses the Florence court order to cease operations on 2018-05-03; the primary collapse trigger remained the February 2018 Nano loss.

records.summary: Cryptocurrency exchange publicly treated as dead after a late-December 2020 hack crisis, with later formal closure announced in January 2021.
records.notes: HEI uses the 2020-12-27 public dead-side marker from Cryptowisser rather than the later 2021-01-18 formal closure announcement. Death reason is mapped to hack because public reporting and the later closure announcement both tied the shutdown to the December 2020 attack. Public sources disagree on origin and establishment details; HEI retains Seychelles from the exchange review and 2013-01-01 as a conservative launch marker from exchange-statistics coverage.

records.summary: Australian cryptocurrency exchange launched in March 2016 that shut down on 2019-10-15 after announcing a purely business-driven closure.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2019-10-15 closure update. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown because public exchange-status coverage described the decision as purely business driven.

records.summary: Canada-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2018 after a scam or rug-pull style failure.
records.notes: Public reporting conflicts on whether the terminal crisis is better characterized as a hack or an exit scam.

records.summary: Taiwan-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2017 that entered a shutdown and account-balance audit period beginning on 2020-01-10 amid bankruptcy and exit-scam rumors.
records.notes: Reuters announced the platform in September 2017, while the live platform launch reference used here is 2017-12-18. Terminal handling uses the 2020-01-10 shutdown/audit notice. Death reason is kept as unknown because public reporting described a shutdown and balance-audit period, with bankruptcy and exit-scam rumors not treated here as a confirmed terminal cause.

records.summary: Italy-linked centralized cryptocurrency exchange that announced it would shut down after a February 2020 hack and asset loss.
records.notes: Public coverage indicates the venue was only a few months old by February 2020. The operational end marker uses the published refund deadline in May 2020.

records.summary: Cryptocurrency exchange launched after the BTC-e seizure and widely described as a successor platform for BTC-e users. WEX stopped functioning in late 2018 amid withdrawal failures, missing customer funds, and later law-enforcement actions involving former WEX leadership.
records.notes: Launch uses the September 2017 rebrand/launch announcement. Death marker uses the late-November 2018 domain-loss point as an operational end marker; exact final shutdown timing remains contested.

records.summary: Thailand-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2019 after a voluntary shutdown.
records.notes: Company statements emphasized closure to pursue other opportunities; some secondary profiles also mention regulatory friction.

records.summary: Singapore-based centralized exchange that appears inactive, with closure details that remain only partially verified.
records.notes: COSS was taken offline on 2020-01-07 and later described acquisition negotiations and a relaunch plan. Because the record is too ambiguous for a clean dead-side classification, it is moved to inactive pending deeper verification.

records.summary: Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2014 after an insolvency-related collapse.
records.notes: Launch uses repeated October 2011 references. Operational end marker uses the March 2014 withdrawal freeze and account-freeze period, which followed earlier hacks and a reserve crisis.

records.summary: India-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2014 after a scam or rug-pull style failure.
Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2018 after a scam or rug-pull style failure.
records.notes: Launch remains approximate because strong primary founding records are still missing. Operational end marker uses the point when users widely reported trading and withdrawals disabled on 2018-01-05.

records.summary: United Kingdom-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2014 after a hack-related crisis.
records.notes: Launch uses a specific February 2014 launch reference. Death marker uses 2014-10-14 when Moolah announced MintPal had been shut down while preparing bankruptcy; later fallout continued after that date.

records.summary: Canada-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2015 after a voluntary shutdown.
records.notes: Launch uses October 2013 reporting on the exchange going live. Closure date and reason come from the January 2015 shutdown announcement specifying a voluntary end rather than insolvency or theft.

records.summary: Finland-based peer-to-peer Bitcoin trading marketplace founded in 2012. LocalBitcoins announced a staged shutdown in February 2023, suspended new trading on 2023-02-16, and left only withdrawal access afterward.
records.notes: Original domain still carries an official closure notice; archive-first display remains appropriate for dead-side history.

records.summary: U.S.-based bitcoin exchange and trading platform acquired by Kraken in January 2016 as part of Kraken's North American expansion alongside CAVIRTEX.
records.notes: Death date uses Kraken's customer-transfer date rather than the initial acquisition announcement date.

records.summary: Canadian bitcoin exchange founded in 2011 and folded into Kraken in January 2016.
records.notes: Death date uses Kraken's customer-transfer date rather than the initial acquisition announcement date.

records.summary: European exchange brand that rebranded from BitBay to Zonda on 2021-11-08.
records.notes: Future successor handling should avoid double-counting Zonda and zondacrypto as separate entities without lineage review.

records.summary: LINE-backed global centralized cryptocurrency exchange operated by LVC USA Inc. It announced a phased voluntary shutdown in late 2022 and scheduled withdrawals to end on 2023-03-31.
records.notes: Entity death date uses the end of withdrawals and service termination rather than the announcement date.

records.summary: Claims-and-derivatives trading platform launched from the CoinFLEX rebrand and shut down in February 2024.
records.notes: Shutdown date is supported by strong reporting, but this retained batch does not include a direct official closure notice; future predecessor and successor review should decide whether CoinFLEX and OPNX remain separate HEI entities or linked lineage records.

records.summary: South Korean cryptocurrency exchange formerly known as Yapizon that shut down and filed for bankruptcy after a second major hack in December 2017.
records.notes: Alias handling preserves the earlier Yapizon identity without treating it as a separate HEI entity. The retained death marker follows same-day shutdown and bankruptcy reporting after the second 2017 hack.

records.summary: Hong Kong-based cryptocurrency exchange founded in 2013 that ceased operations after a court-ordered winding-up and compulsory liquidation process in March 2019.
records.notes: Operational stop follows the 2019-03-13 winding-up order. Liquidators were formally appointed on 2019-03-20. This upgrade preserves the liquidation basis and adds archive/status corroboration while retaining the insolvency classification.

records.summary: Indian centralized exchange that permanently disabled trading on 2019-06-27 amid regulatory uncertainty and disruption.
records.notes: The retained death marker follows Koinex's 2019-06-27 permanent trading shutdown announcement. Launch date remains year-level approximate pending stronger founding evidence.

records.summary: United Kingdom bitcoin exchange brand whose customer base and domains were acquired by CoinCorner in October 2021.
records.notes: Official successor-side support materials state that CoinCorner acquired Coinfloor in October 2021 and migrated or closed legacy accounts.

records.summary: Dutch bitcoin exchange founded in 2013 and folded into Kraken following a June 2016 acquisition and account transfer.
records.notes: Entity death_date uses the customer-transfer completion date after Kraken's 2016-06-28 acquisition announcement.

records.summary: South Korean centralized exchange that began its shutdown process in April 2019 and required users to withdraw assets by the end of the month.
records.notes: Closure is supported by multiple contemporaneous reports quoting the official site notice. Cause remains mixed across declining volume, regulatory background, and business decisions, so death_reason stays unknown.

records.summary: Ukraine-linked centralized exchange that announced its shutdown on 2019-01-28 and gave users a 30 day withdrawal window.
records.notes: Shutdown language cited insufficient liquidity, but this batch keeps death_reason as unknown. A 2025 official retrospective Medium post confirms that a shutdown announcement was issued in January 2019.

records.summary: Altcoin-focused exchange that announced a shutdown in late September 2019 and ceased trading and API access on 2019-10-07.
records.notes: The closure schedule is well supported, but the cause is kept as unknown to avoid overcommitting on business-decision language preserved through third-party mirrors and listings.

records.summary: Vancouver-based centralized exchange that entered court-supervised receivership in November 2019 after customer-access failures and large asset shortfalls.
records.notes: The shutdown path involves receivership, asset shortfalls, and later fraud findings, so the retained death_reason remains unknown.

records.summary: Italian centralized exchange that interrupted operations in February 2023 due to liquidity problems and later entered judicial liquidation in Milan.
records.notes: Operational death marker uses the 2023-02-17 interruption of platform operations. Later court-supervised insolvency and judicial liquidation are documented separately in evidence.

records.summary: Austrian virtual-world and bitcoin exchange that announced an end of service in late 2019 and permanently closed on 2020-01-06.
records.notes: Closure timing is clear, but the detailed reason is not strongly primary-sourced in this pass; status remains dead with a conservative voluntary shutdown classification.

records.summary: Polish exchange that went offline in October 2016 after reporting external interference and major asset losses.
records.notes: Uses the October 2016 operational stop. Investigation context is preserved in evidence.

records.summary: Early U.S. bitcoin exchange that shut down in 2012, relaunched, then halted trading again in 2013 because of banking and regulatory issues.
records.notes: The final operational end uses the August 2013 halt rather than the earlier 2012 shutdown.

records.summary: U.S. bitcoin exchange whose operators were charged for running an unlawful exchange and money-laundering scheme.
records.notes: Uses the July 2015 enforcement-side operational end marker.

records.summary: Early UK bitcoin exchange that progressively shut down markets in 2012 and ceased operations after banking problems and shrinking volume.
records.notes: Uses the 2012-12-19 GBP market closure as the terminal marker.

records.summary: Regional Binance fiat surface for Ugandan shillings that shut down in November 2020 as Binance consolidated users onto Binance.com.
records.notes: Lineage-sensitive regional surface. Keep separate from Binance.com.

records.summary: Polish exchange that suffered catastrophic wallet data loss in July 2011 and was acquired by Mt. Gox in August 2011.
records.notes: This record belongs on an acquired-lineage track with Mt. Gox as successor.

records.summary: Canadian exchange that announced an immediate shutdown in March 2016 after an alleged hack and wallet deficiency.
records.notes: Uses the exchange's immediate shutdown notice as the death marker.

records.summary: Chinese exchange that announced the closure of trading operations during the 2017 domestic crackdown.
records.notes: Uses the September 2017 closure announcement during China's exchange crackdown.

records.summary: Regional Binance-branded fiat exchange that shut down at the end of November 2020 after Binance expanded fiat access on its main platform.
records.notes: Lineage-sensitive regional surface. Keep separate from Binance.com.

records.summary: Singapore-local Binance affiliate that wound down and closed after the operator withdrew its licensing effort in the country.
records.notes: Lineage-sensitive regional surface. Keep separate from Binance.com.

records.summary: United States bitcoin exchange that later ceased operations after prolonged banking disruption and final Georgia regulatory action.
records.notes: ACH and wire transfers were halted in early 2014 because of banking and regulatory uncertainty. A Georgia cease and desist order became final on 2018-07-25.

records.summary: BitShares-based decentralized exchange that announced closure in December 2019, citing market conditions and increasing regulation pressure.
records.notes: Shutdown notice said deposits would close on 2019-12-03 and all services and servers would terminate after 2019-12-15.

records.summary: Polish cryptocurrency exchange that shut down in April 2019 after earlier KNF warning-list action, with later reporting alleging customer-fund losses.

records.summary: Ireland-registered cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2016 that disappeared from the web in June 2019 after withdrawal failures and was publicly treated as an exit-scam style dead exchange.

Death reason is mapped to scam_rug because public reporting and the exchange review describe classic exit-scam characteristics after blocked withdrawals and deletion of the website and social accounts.

records.summary: China-linked centralized cryptocurrency exchange that operated under the Bter brand before the Gate.io successor brand became the continuing exchange identity.

records.summary: United Kingdom cryptocurrency exchange that was later marked inactive and publicly described as having closed operations by 2019-04-01.

records.summary: Russian cryptocurrency exchange that became inaccessible by late June 2019 and was subsequently marked dead, although some later status pages still described it as suspended for reorganization.

records.summary: Advanced trading surface under Coinbase that was replaced by Coinbase Advanced, with customer history access ending after December 2023.

records.summary: China-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2013 that was publicly marked inaccessible and dead by mid-June 2019.

records.summary: China-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly marked inaccessible and dead on 2021-07-15.
records.notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2021-07-15 dead-side update.

records.summary: Canada-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly described as closed in early 2025, with user inquiries ending on 2025-04-01.

records.summary: Australian centralized cryptocurrency exchange platform operated by Blockchain Global. ACX ceased operations abruptly in late 2019, leaving customers unable to withdraw assets, and Blockchain Global later entered voluntary administration and liquidation.
records.notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2021-07-15 dead-side update.

records.summary: Polish cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly described as inaccessible and dead by 2021-11-11.
records.notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2021-11-11 dead-side update.

records.notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2023-01-11 dead-side update. The 'new platform' notice is treated as context, not proof of a successful successor.

records.summary: Russian cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly described as dead by 2019-07-18 after volume-reporting ceased, the site presented an invalid certificate, and trading activity appeared stale.
records.notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Cryptowisser review update uses 2019-07-18 as the dead-side marker, while Cryptowisser graveyard shows 2019-07-17 with business reasons.

records.summary: Crypto exchange branded as ZT that was publicly described as inaccessible and dead by 2024-09-18, with CoinMarketCap later showing that withdrawals and trading were suspended.

records.summary: Cryptocurrency exchange branded as AAX that suspended withdrawals in November 2022 and was publicly marked dead by 2023-01-11.
records.notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2023-01-11 dead-side update. Source coverage shows a 2022-11-13 suspension first, then a later dead-side marker.

records.summary: Greece-linked non-custodial exchange or broker-style swap platform that was publicly described as inaccessible and dead by 2024-09-18.
records.notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2024-09-18 dead-side update. Launch year is 2019, recorded conservatively as 2019-01-01.

records.summary: BNB Chain-born decentralized exchange and DeFi protocol family launched in 2020 and still active as a multichain DEX stack under the PancakeSwap brand.
records.notes: Entity-level umbrella record. Official materials state PancakeSwap launched in 2020, but this pass does not pin a stronger official day-level launch marker. Deployment-level and product-level modeling are still pending.

records.summary: Bitcoin-focused peer-to-peer decentralized exchange network that went into production in 2016 and remains active through Bisq 1 and Bisq 2.
records.notes: Entity-level umbrella record. Launch marker uses the documented production date. Bisq 1 and Bisq 2 are not yet split into separate protocol or product records.

records.summary: Dutch cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly marked closed on 2020-09-01 after citing Dutch registration requirements and operating-cost pressure.
records.notes: Cause stays conservative as unknown. Public closure text references Dutch registration requirements and economic infeasibility, but HEI does not hard-classify this beyond the terminal closure marker.

records.summary: Brazil-linked cryptocurrency exchange associated with XRP trading that is now permanently closed according to public exchange-status sources.
records.notes: Cause stays conservative as unknown. Public handling relies on exchange-status coverage stating the platform is permanently closed and that a closure notice was shown on the website.

records.summary: Polish cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly marked dead on 2021-07-15 after its website became inaccessible with no preceding maintenance or migration notice.
records.notes: Cause stays conservative as unknown. Public handling relies on the dead-side status update tied to site inaccessibility on 2021-07-15.

records.summary: Privacy-focused non-KYC crypto exchange that announced it would cease operations on May 1, 2025 amid allegations that it processed funds tied to the Bybit hack and amid reported transatlantic law-enforcement pressure.
records.notes: Death reason is regulation rather than voluntary_shutdown because the shutdown was reported in direct connection with laundering allegations, a claimed transatlantic law-enforcement operation, and possible criminal charges. Keep allegations carefully worded; sources report that eXch denied being a mixer but acknowledged processing a small portion of Bybit-hack funds.

records.summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2014 that was publicly reported bankrupt on 2021-01-12 and is no longer operating.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-01-12 bankruptcy update. Death reason is set to insolvency based on the bankruptcy filing and dead-side treatment.

records.summary: Seychelles-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly treated as dead on 2023-01-03 after disappearing from normal operation.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2023-01-03 graveyard marker. Cause stays conservative as unknown even though public status coverage characterizes the exchange as having disappeared.

records.summary: Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2017 that was publicly treated as closed on 2021-01-01 amid regulation-linked shutdown handling.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-01-01 dead-side update. Death reason is set to regulation in line with public exchange-status coverage.

records.summary: Estonia-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly treated as dead on 2021-01-01 for business-related shutdown reasons.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-01-01 graveyard marker. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown from business-reasons style closure handling.

records.summary: South Korean cryptocurrency exchange launched in March 2018 that closed all exchange services on 2021-03-22 and was publicly treated as a business-reasons shutdown.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-03-22 dead-side update. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown because public exchange-status coverage says no specific reason was given and assumes business reasons.

records.summary: Slovakia-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2019 that paused operations on 2021-04-22 after a 2020 hack crisis and was subsequently treated as dead.

records.summary: Hong Kong-based cryptocurrency exchange active since 2017 that was publicly marked dead in late 2021 after prolonged financial troubles and business-driven closure handling.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-12-02 public dead-side update. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown from business-reasons closure handling. Cryptowisser's graveyard also places Coinsuper in the 2021 business-reasons cohort.

records.summary: United States-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2018 that announced closure in November 2020 and was publicly treated as dead by the end of 2020 under business-reasons shutdown handling.

Some SurfTheWeb materials say the exchange was developed or operational since late 2017, but HEI retains 2018-01-01 as the conservative public launch marker.

records.summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2015 that stopped providing services on 2020-03-30 and was subsequently treated as dead.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2020-03-30 closure update. Public review coverage confirms United Kingdom domicile and 2015 launch, while the preserved closure message cites technical problems and a final withdrawal deadline but does not support a cleaner cause classification than unknown.

records.summary: Estonia-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in August 2019 that announced closure of its centralized exchange on 2024-12-18 for economic reasons and was subsequently treated as dead.

records.summary: Isle of Man-based cryptocurrency exchange that publicly closed its retail service on 2023-01-24 in order to focus on B2B operations and was subsequently treated as dead-side.

Public review coverage describes the exchange as from Isle of Man and active since 2017, while the preserved launch press release shows a January 2018 live launch; HEI uses 2018-01-01 as the conservative public launch marker.

records.summary: Hong Kong-based cryptocurrency exchange active since May 2018 that was marked scam and moved to dead-side on 2022-08-17 after widespread withdrawal and account-blocking complaints.

records.summary: Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in January 2018 that was publicly marked as a scam and moved to dead-side handling on 2019-11-01 after widespread accusations from users.

HEI uses 2018-01-01 as the conservative public launch marker based on January 2018 launch descriptions.

records.summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange that began operating in 2015 and shut down all services on 2020-03-01 after stating that it was no longer economically feasible to continue operations.

records.summary: Mongolia-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2017 that was publicly treated as dead by 2020-03-31 after suspended withdrawals, a missing CEO, and widespread exit-scam handling.

HEI uses Mongolia and 2017-01-01 as conservative canonical markers.

records.summary: Russia-based centralized cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2020 that was publicly treated as dead on 2024-09-18 after the website became inaccessible with no preceding maintenance or migration notice.

records.summary: Hong Kong-registered cryptocurrency platform launched in 2016 that was shut down on 2023-01-18 after U.S. and French enforcement action tied to illicit-finance allegations.

records.summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in May 2019 that was publicly treated as dead on 2022-12-02 after the platform became inaccessible and showed no clear operational activity.

records.summary: Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange active since 2017 that was publicly treated as dead on 2022-02-01 after its website became inaccessible without any preceding maintenance or migration notice.

records.summary: Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in May 2018 that was acquired and rebranded as Bithumb Singapore on 2019-08-08.

records.summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange active since 2017 that was publicly treated as dead on 2021-07-15 after its website became inaccessible without any preceding maintenance or migration notice.

records.summary: Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in March 2018 that was publicly treated as dead on 2021-03-11 after its website became inaccessible without any preceding maintenance or continuity notice.

records.summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in January 2017 that was publicly treated as dead on 2021-04-10 after its website became inaccessible without any maintenance or continuity notice.

Cause stays conservative as unknown because public status coverage only confirms that the website was inaccessible, with no preceding maintenance or replacement-site notice. HEI keeps United Kingdom as the canonical origin marker from the public exchange review.

records.summary: Turkey-based cryptocurrency exchange founded in 2017 that announced it had ceased all activities on 2021-04-26 after financial strain and was subsequently treated as dead.

HEI keeps the closure-note date as the terminal marker rather than the later reporting and investigation timeline.

records.summary: China-linked cryptocurrency exchange launched in December 2016 that was publicly treated as dead on 2021-05-10 after announcing that trading operations had ceased.

Death reason is mapped to regulation because Cryptowisser's graveyard classifies the exchange under regulatory reasons, while the review page states that the exchange had closed down and reproduced a site notice that trading had ceased. HEI keeps China as the canonical country marker from Cryptowisser and the graveyard flag, while later directory pages also describe Hong Kong operating context.

records.summary: Turkey-based cryptocurrency exchange active since 2018 that was publicly treated as dead on 2021-10-11 after reports that it had shut down amid increased regulatory pressure from Turkish authorities.

records.summary: Polish centralized cryptocurrency exchange that abruptly ceased operations in July 2019, citing loss of liquidity and inability to continue service.
records.notes: Terminal handling uses the 2019-07-08 public closure marker. Death reason is mapped to insolvency because the platform notice and secondary reporting cite loss of liquidity. Public notices refer to Bitmarket.pl/net; HEI keeps bitmarket.pl as the canonical original-domain marker.

records.summary: Denmark-based BitShares decentralized exchange platform officially launched in October 2015 and closed down on 2020-05-15 after an exchange closure notice.
