records.notes: Existing Bybit entity remains active. This event should not be interpreted as a global shutdown.

records.title: Bybit closed its ETH reserve gap after the hack
records.description: Bybit said or was reported to have closed the ETH gap after the hack by acquiring or securing large amounts of ETH, restoring 1:1 backing and continuing operations.
records.notes: Recovery/reserve-gap event supports active classification despite the critical hack.

records.title: DMM Bitcoin suffered an unauthorized BTC outflow
records.description: DMM Bitcoin suffered an unauthorized outflow of bitcoin in 2024, widely reported as approximately 4,502.9 BTC and roughly JPY 48 billion at the time.
records.notes: Use hack because the outflow is the causative security incident behind the later transfer and shutdown.

records.title: DMM Bitcoin announced customer transfer to SBI VC Trade
records.description: DMM Bitcoin announced an agreement to transfer customer accounts and assets to SBI VC Trade, with the transfer path replacing a restart of DMM Bitcoin's own exchange service.
records.notes: This is the practical shutdown-announcement marker; use official DMM/SBI sources for final staging validation.

records.title: DMM Bitcoin service ended after SBI VC Trade transfer
records.description: DMM Bitcoin service ended on March 8, 2025 after customer assets and accounts were transferred to SBI VC Trade.
records.notes: HEI uses the official service-end / transfer completion date as the terminal marker.

records.title: BtcTurk saw suspicious hot-wallet outflows
records.description: BtcTurk experienced suspicious outflows from hot wallets in August 2025, with blockchain/security reporting and news sources estimating the affected value around USD 48-49 million.
records.notes: Use careful language because some sources frame it as suspicious outflows / hack fears, while the exchange described security measures and user-asset safety.

records.title: BtcTurk suspended crypto deposits and withdrawals
records.description: BtcTurk suspended cryptocurrency deposits and withdrawals after the suspicious hot-wallet activity, while reports indicated Turkish-lira services and trading remained available.
records.notes: Temporary crypto-transfer suspension; not a shutdown marker.

records.title: GMX v1 / GLP was exploited for about USD 42 million
records.description: GMX v1 / GLP on Arbitrum was exploited for about USD 42 million, with the attacker later offered a white-hat bounty and some funds reportedly returned.
records.notes: Use exploit because this was a protocol/liquidity-pool exploit, not a centralized exchange wallet hack.

records.title: GMX disabled v1 trading and GLP mint/redeem
records.description: GMX disabled GMX v1 trading and GLP mint/redeem functionality on Arbitrum and Avalanche after the exploit, while stating or reporting that GMX v2 was unaffected.
records.notes: Temporary/partial protocol disablement; keep entity active because the broader GMX protocol continued.

records.title: GMX recovery process began after white-hat negotiations
records.description: Follow-up reporting described a white-hat bounty / return-of-funds process after the GMX exploit, supporting active classification despite the serious v1 incident.
records.notes: Modeled as other because HEI has no white-hat recovery enum.

records.title: Bithumb accidentally credited users with massive BTC balances
records.description: Bithumb mistakenly recorded approximately 620,000 BTC as promotional rewards in user accounts, a ledger/payout error widely reported as equivalent to tens of billions of U.S. dollars at the time.
records.notes: Modeled as other because HEI has no dedicated operational_error enum. This was not reported as a hack.

records.title: Bithumb restricted affected accounts after the crediting error
records.description: Bithumb restricted affected users' trading and withdrawals within about 35 minutes of the erroneous crediting incident and later recovered the vast majority of the mistaken balances.
records.notes: Modeled as withdrawal_suspended because the status-changing user impact involved account and withdrawal restrictions, not global exchange closure.

records.title: Bithumb incident prompted regulatory inspection
records.description: South Korean regulators and authorities were reported to be inspecting Bithumb after the erroneous BTC crediting incident exposed internal-control and operational-risk concerns.
records.notes: Regulatory inspection / scrutiny context; keep entity active.

records.title: WOO X reported unauthorized withdrawals from nine accounts
records.description: WOO X reported a contained incident in which nine user accounts experienced unauthorized withdrawals totaling about USD 14 million, while saying the affected users would be fully covered.
records.notes: Use hack as the nearest HEI enum for an unauthorized-withdrawal breach; wording should preserve that the incident was account-level and contained according to WOO X.

records.title: WOO X temporarily paused withdrawals
records.description: As a precaution after the unauthorized-withdrawal incident, WOO X temporarily suspended withdrawals while completing an internal review and coordinating with security teams and other exchanges.
records.notes: Temporary withdrawal pause only; not a shutdown or global trading halt.

records.title: WOO X committed to cover all unauthorized withdrawals
records.description: WOO X said affected users had been contacted and that all unauthorized withdrawals would be fully covered, supporting active-side treatment rather than dead or insolvent classification.
records.notes: Modeled as other because HEI has no compensation_commitment enum.

records.title: Crypto-Trade announced permanent shutdown
records.description: Crypto-Trade announced that it would shut down permanently because low volume and a weak userbase left it unable to cover server costs, staff salaries, and promotion costs.
records.notes: The quoted announcement gave users a short withdrawal window.

records.title: Crypto-Trade reached its planned offline date
records.description: Crypto-Trade's quoted shutdown notice said the site would go offline on 2015-01-29 at 06:00 UTC after processing pending withdrawals.
records.notes: HEI uses the planned site-offline date as the terminal marker.

records.title: SwissCEX public announcement thread opened
records.description: SwissCEX publicly described itself as a Switzerland-based cryptocurrency exchange operating since February 2014.
records.notes: Launch marker uses the public announcement thread rather than a formal company registration record.

records.title: SwissCEX closure process was publicly discussed
records.description: The SwissCEX operator referenced an exchange-closure document and said users would receive private keys for settled accounts as part of the closure process.
records.notes: The date follows quoted closure-process discussion in the public Bitcointalk thread.

records.title: SwissCEX was reported closed
records.description: Public forum discussion reported that the SwissCEX site had closed while users were receiving private keys for coins held on the exchange.
records.notes: HEI uses the public site-closed discussion as the terminal marker, supported by later inactive market-data listings.

records.title: CryptalDash launch marker
records.description: Public exchange-review coverage describes CryptalDash as a cryptocurrency exchange launched around July 2018.
records.notes: HEI uses a month-level launch marker from secondary exchange-review coverage.

records.title: CryptalDash rebranded into DLTify
records.description: Public exchange-status coverage stated that CryptalDash was no longer active under the CryptalDash name and had rebranded into DLTify.
records.notes: HEI treats this as the terminal marker for the original CryptalDash exchange identity.

records.title: FCoin paused trading
records.description: FCoin paused trading after disclosing that its asset reserve had fallen short of user liabilities.
records.notes: CoinDesk reported that FCoin had paused trading and withdrawals while revealing the asset shortfall.

records.title: FCoin could no longer process user withdrawals
records.description: FCoin disclosed that it was unable to process user withdrawal demands because its asset reserve was below liabilities.
records.notes: The disclosed shortfall was estimated at roughly 7,000 to 13,000 BTC.

records.title: FCoin disclosed a major BTC shortfall
records.description: FCoin disclosed a reserve shortfall estimated at 7,000 to 13,000 BTC, creating an insolvency / withdrawal-impediment state for the exchange.
records.notes: HEI uses this disclosure date as the terminal marker for the FCoin exchange record.

records.title: CoinedUp public-use marker
records.description: Public forum discussion in early January 2014 shows users discussing and using coinedup.com as an altcoin exchange.
records.notes: HEI uses a conservative year-start marker because the current evidence shows use in early January 2014 but does not establish a precise launch date.

records.title: CoinedUp service reduction was publicly observed
records.description: Public forum discussion observed CoinedUp reduced to a small number of listed coins and speculated that the exchange might be closing or restructuring.
records.notes: This is not treated as a shutdown_effective event because it is community observation rather than an official terminal notice.

records.title: Atomic Trade went live
records.description: Public forum discussion described Atomic-Trade.com as a United States exchange and stated that it went live on 2014-02-05.
records.notes: HEI uses the explicit 2014-02-05 live-date claim from public Bitcointalk discussion.

records.title: Atomic Trade was bought out by Banx.io
records.description: Public acquisition coverage stated that Banx.io bought out Atomic-Trade.com and that Atomic-Trade.com became known as Banx.io as of 2015-02-23.
records.notes: HEI treats this as the terminal marker for the original Atomic Trade exchange identity.

records.title: LazyCoins beta launch marker
records.description: Public forum and trading references show LazyCoins operating as an exchange from July 2014, with the announcement thread describing a beta launch on 2014-07-21.
records.notes: HEI uses the beta-launch marker rather than the later official-launch publicity date.

records.title: LazyCoins relaunch and LazyPay expansion were reported
records.description: Public reporting described LazyCoins as relaunching its online exchange and expanding toward LazyPay mobile wallet and merchant services.
records.notes: Recorded as contextual operational expansion rather than a status transition.

records.title: LazyCoins official launch was reported
records.description: Cointelegraph reported LazyCoins' official launch as a UK-based digital currency exchange supporting GBP and euro markets.
records.notes: This is retained as a later official-launch publicity marker after the beta / relaunch phase.

records.title: 796 Exchange public launch marker
records.description: 796 Exchange publicly described itself as a Hong Kong-operated Bitcoin futures and finance exchange and referenced public launch / trading activity beginning around 2013-06-09.
records.notes: The English announcement thread describes 796 Xchange Limited, the 796.com domain, Hong Kong incorporation, and trading activity just after 2013-06-09. The Chinese announcement describes the internal test period before the public launch.

records.title: 796 Exchange reported 1,000 BTC withdrawal-address tampering loss
records.description: Public reporting described a 1,000 BTC loss at 796 after a withdrawal address was allegedly tampered with during a compromised customer-service / withdrawal process.
records.notes: HEI records this as a major incident, not as a shutdown marker.

records.title: BitKonan founded / launched
records.description: BitKonan is described by multiple references as a Croatia-based centralized cryptocurrency exchange founded or launched in 2013.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Cryptowisser and Blank describe 2013; CoinPaprika describes the exchange as running since 2014.

records.title: Bitcoin-24 first live
records.description: Bitcoin-24 is described by Bitcoin Wiki as first live in May 2012.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2012-05-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Bitcoin-24 shut down after bank-account closures
records.description: Bitcoin-24 displayed a shutdown / unavailable notice in April 2013, citing closure of its Polish bank account, while later reporting described both Polish and German account closures and legal action.
records.notes: Used as the terminal regulatory / banking shutdown marker.

records.title: Bitfloor operating since 2011
records.description: Bitfloor is described as a New York-based Bitcoin exchange operating since 2011, with first publicity in February 2012.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2011-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Bitfloor lost about 24,000 BTC in hot-wallet theft
records.description: Bitfloor suspended operations after attackers compromised servers and stole roughly 24,000 BTC from an unencrypted wallet-key backup.
records.notes: Major incident. Bitfloor later resumed operations, so this is not used as the terminal shutdown marker.

records.title: Bitfloor halted operations indefinitely
records.description: Bitfloor announced it would halt operations and return funds after its U.S. bank account was scheduled to be closed and it could no longer provide the same level of USD deposits and withdrawals.
records.notes: Used as the terminal shutdown marker.

records.title: Harborly founded
records.description: Harborly is described as a Texas-based online Bitcoin platform founded in 2014.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2014-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Harborly announced shutdown
records.description: Harborly said it was closing to focus on a separate project and that the closure was not caused by a hack, fraud, or security incident.
records.notes: Used as the terminal voluntary shutdown marker. CoinDesk published its report on 2015-08-20 and refers to a 2015-08-14 Harborly blog post.

records.title: Igot founded
records.description: Igot is described by ABC and CoinTelegraph reporting as an Australian-founded Bitcoin exchange founded in 2013.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2013-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Igot customers reported unpaid withdrawals and collapse risk
records.description: ABC reported that Igot appeared on the verge of collapse, with customers owed funds, months-long withdrawal failures, and the owner acknowledging difficulty paying customers back.
records.notes: Recorded as severe operational distress / withdrawal failure, not as a confirmed shutdown marker.

records.title: Bitparking exchange operating by 2012
records.description: Public forum and Q&A references show exchange.bitparking.com operating as a Namecoin / Bitcoin exchange by 2012.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2012-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Bitparking exchange moved toward withdrawal-only recovery
records.description: Forum-preserved exchange status text stated that the exchange suffered drive corruption, would be brought up for withdrawals only after balance recovery, and that the exchange would cease operation.
records.notes: Recorded as a severe operational failure / withdrawal-only transition, not a fully classified terminal shutdown with death_reason.

records.title: BTCChina founded
records.description: BTCChina is described as China's first Bitcoin exchange and as founded in June 2011.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2011-06-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BTCChina rebranded to BTCC
records.description: BTCC announced that BTCChina was rebranding to BTCC to support a more global identity and broader product set.
records.notes: Used as the terminal brand marker for the BTCChina historical record.

records.title: BitNZ announced
records.description: Bitcoin Wiki records BitNZ as announced on 2011-09-12 as a New Zealand BTC/NZD exchange.
records.notes: Used as the launch / public announcement marker.

records.title: BitNZ withdrawal deadline after closure announcement
records.description: BitNZ announced it would close due to New Zealand banking problems and asked customers to withdraw funds by 2017-04-15.
records.notes: Used as the terminal voluntary shutdown marker. The closure context is banking-access loss rather than a hack.

records.title: Coinrail established
records.description: Coinrail is described by market-data trackers as a South Korea-registered centralized exchange established in 2017.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2017-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Coinrail suffered major cyber intrusion
records.description: Coinrail suspended services after a cyber intrusion in June 2018, with public reporting estimating losses around $40 million in cryptocurrencies.
records.notes: Recorded as a major hack / suspension event. It is not used as a definitive shutdown marker because later references indicate service resumption attempts.

records.title: Bitcoin-Central launched
records.description: Bitcoin Wiki records Bitcoin-Central as launched on 2010-12-29.
records.notes: Used as the historical launch marker.

records.title: Bitcoin-Central announced regulated banking framework
records.description: Bitcoin-Central / Paymium announced that Bitcoin-Central would operate within a European regulatory framework with funds held at a partner financial institution.
records.notes: Positive regulatory / banking milestone, not a shutdown marker.

records.title: Bitcoin-Central relaunched after months-long closure
records.description: Bitcoin Wiki records that Bitcoin-Central relaunched on 2013-09-25 after being closed for roughly half a year.
records.notes: Recorded as a relaunch / recovery marker. This is not a terminal event.

records.title: Coinffeine officially launched
records.description: Coinffeine officially launched its decentralized peer-to-peer Bitcoin exchange in more than 70 countries.
records.notes: Used as the public launch marker.

records.title: Coinffeine active development / operations appear to have ceased
records.description: Later profile references describe Coinffeine as having ceased active development and operations around 2016-2017.
records.notes: Approximate year-level marker only. This is not treated as a definitive shutdown date.

records.title: GLBSE launched
records.description: GLBSE launched in 2011 as a Bitcoin-denominated asset and securities exchange for shares, bonds, and other Bitcoin economy assets.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Bitcoin Wiki records testing in April 2011 and launch soon after; Bitcoin Magazine describes launch in May 2011.

records.title: GLBSE abruptly closed
records.description: GLBSE abruptly closed in October 2012. Contemporary reporting and community explanations cite legal, AML, tax, and regulatory concerns as the core closure rationale.
records.notes: Used as the terminal closure marker. Some public records preserve later customer-claim and refund-process updates after the initial closure.

records.title: BTCQuick launched
records.description: Contemporary reporting described BTCQuick as launched in November 2012 and as having reached nearly $2 million in bitcoin sales by October 2013.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2012-11-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BitFunder launched
records.description: Contemporary reporting describes BitFunder as launched around December 2012 as a Bitcoin-denominated stock / securities exchange.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2012-12-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BitFunder ceased new trading
records.description: BitFunder announced that from 2013-11-14 users would no longer be able to enter new positions or sell positions on the website, with remaining bitcoin balances to be transferred later.
records.notes: Used as the terminal trading-shutdown marker. Later SEC and DOJ actions are retained as regulatory / fraud context.

records.title: LocalEthereum launched
records.description: LocalCryptos began in 2017 as LocalEthereum, a non-custodial peer-to-peer Ethereum marketplace.
records.notes: Used as the original platform launch marker.

records.title: LocalEthereum rebranded to LocalCryptos
records.description: LocalEthereum rebranded to LocalCryptos and added non-custodial peer-to-peer Bitcoin trading support.
records.notes: Rebrand / expansion event, not a terminal event.

records.title: LocalCryptos disabled new trade creation
records.description: LocalCryptos announced a voluntary wind-down in October 2022, with new account creation disabled after 2022-11-04 and new trade creation disabled after 2022-11-18.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marketplace shutdown marker. Existing wallet UI availability is not treated as continued exchange operation.

records.title: Bit-Z founded
records.description: Market-data and company-profile sources describe BitZ / Bit-Z as founded or launched in 2016.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2016-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BitZ scheduled final platform shutdown
records.description: BitZ announced that it would stop spot trading and disable website / app login on 2021-10-21 as part of an orderly exit following Chinese regulatory policy requirements.
records.notes: Used as the terminal regulatory shutdown marker.

records.title: Crex24 launched
records.description: Exchange profile references describe Crex24 as launched around 2017, with one profile giving 2017-08 as the launch marker.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2017-08-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Crex24 has no active tracked markets
records.description: Current exchange trackers show Crex24 with no available market data, no tracked volume, or as no longer operational.
records.notes: Recorded as a current inactive / no-market-data status marker, not as a confirmed terminal shutdown date.

records.title: CHBTC founded
records.description: Profile and exchange-history sources describe CHBTC as a China-based exchange founded in 2013, with one source giving June 2013.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2013-06-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: CHBTC ceased China-market trading activity
records.description: Profile sources describe CHBTC as ceasing trading activity on 2017-09-30 due to Chinese regulatory compliance / pressure.
records.notes: Recorded as the China-market regulatory stop, not the final brand-transition marker.

records.title: CHBTC transitioned to ZB.com
records.description: Several profile sources describe CHBTC as changing its name to ZB.com and focusing on international operations after the China-market shutdown.
records.notes: Used as the terminal brand-transition marker for the CHBTC historical record.

records.title: DragonEX launched
records.description: CoinMarketCap describes DragonEX as launched on 2017-11-02 in Singapore.
records.notes: Used as the launch marker.

records.title: DragonEX suffered a major cyberattack
records.description: DragonEX announced that user and platform crypto assets were transferred and stolen in a March 2019 cyberattack, and temporarily closed platform services while investigating the incident.
records.notes: Recorded as a major hack / service disruption event, not as a confirmed terminal shutdown.

records.title: CoinGather launched
records.description: CoinGather announced itself as an alternate cryptocurrency exchange in 2014 and later DOJ forfeiture material also described CoinGather as launched in 2014.
records.notes: Date follows the Bitcointalk announcement month. Stored as 2014-08-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: CoinGather abruptly went offline
records.description: CoinGather went offline in late November 2017 after withdrawal complaints and later became the subject of FBI / DOJ seizure and forfeiture activity involving cryptocurrency wallets accessible through the exchange.
records.notes: Used as the terminal disappearance marker. DOJ forfeiture material is recorded as post-shutdown legal context.

records.title: Altilly launched
records.description: Exchange profile references describe Altilly as a centralized exchange established around 2018.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2018-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Altilly suffered a major security breach
records.description: Public exchange trackers and reviews describe Altilly as having suffered a late-2020 security breach, after which the service became inaccessible or inactive.
records.notes: Recorded as a major breach / service-disruption marker, not as a definitive terminal shutdown date.

records.title: BitMarket.eu announced
records.description: Bitcoin Wiki records BitMarket.eu as announced on 2011-04-05 as a multi-currency Bitcoin exchange / order-matching market.
records.notes: Used as the public launch / announcement marker.

records.title: BitMarket.eu disclosed major customer-fund loss
records.description: The operator disclosed that customer funds had been used for speculation and that nearly 20,000 BTC of customer funds had been lost.
records.notes: Recorded as the insolvency / balance-freeze marker before final closure.

records.title: BitMarket.eu operations closed down
records.description: Investor communications in the Bitcointalk thread stated that BitMarket.eu operations would be closed down, leaving the site only to handle old frozen coin issues.
records.notes: Used as the terminal operations-closed marker.

records.title: Coinapult founded
records.description: Contemporary reporting describes Coinapult as founded around 2012 by Ira Miller and Erik Voorhees.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2012-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Coinapult suffered 150 BTC hot-wallet compromise
records.description: Coinapult announced a hot-wallet compromise in March 2015 that resulted in the loss of 150 BTC. Reporting stated customer funds were not affected and the service later restored operations.
records.notes: Recorded as a major security incident, not as a terminal shutdown marker.

records.title: Stocks.exchange / STEX launched
records.description: Exchange profile references describe STEX, previously Stocks.exchange, as launched around 2017-02.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2017-02-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Stocks.exchange / STEX has no active tracked markets
records.description: Current exchange trackers show no available trading-pair / volume data or mark STEX as no longer operational.
records.notes: Recorded as a current inactive / no-market-data status marker, not as a confirmed shutdown date.

records.title: SouthXchange founded
records.description: Exchange tracker references describe SouthXchange as an Argentina-based centralized cryptocurrency exchange founded around 2015.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2015-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: SouthXchange deactivated deposits and trading
records.description: Tracker references preserve SouthXchange's wind-down notice stating that operational activities were concluding and deposits and trading were deactivated.
records.notes: The 2024-01-15 date is used as the trading-disabled marker based on tracker summaries; the final withdrawal deadline is recorded separately.

records.title: SouthXchange final withdrawal deadline
records.description: SouthXchange users were instructed to withdraw remaining funds before 2024-01-31 as the exchange concluded all operational activities.
records.notes: Used as the terminal operational shutdown marker.

records.title: AEX launched
records.description: Exchange tracker references describe AEX as one of the early centralized digital asset exchanges, founded around 2013-08.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2013-08-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: AEX closed withdrawal function amid liquidity pressure
records.description: AEX reportedly closed the withdrawal function because short-term liquidity problems had not been resolved and the platform could not meet withdrawal demand in the short term.
records.notes: Recorded as the liquidity / withdrawal freeze marker before the later full service suspension.

records.title: AEX suspended all platform services
records.description: AEX announced that all platform-related services would be suspended from 2022-07-17 while it cooperated with a Guangxi police investigation.
records.notes: Used as the terminal platform service suspension marker.

records.title: Beaxy launched
records.description: Beaxy Exchange launched as a centralized cryptocurrency exchange in June 2019.
records.notes: CoinMarketCap lists 2019-06-10; The Block reported Beaxy opening on 2019-06-11 after a delayed launch.

records.title: Beaxy shut down amid SEC enforcement action
records.description: Beaxy ceased operations after the U.S. SEC charged the platform and associated parties with operating as an unregistered exchange, broker, and clearing agency.
records.notes: Used as the terminal exchange shutdown marker.

records.title: Dasset founded
records.description: Exchange tracker and industry references describe Dasset as a New Zealand digital asset exchange founded in 2017.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2017-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Dasset entered liquidation
records.description: Digital Asset Exchange Limited, trading as Dasset, entered liquidation on 2023-08-14 after customers had been unable to access funds and the company had been unable to secure replacement banking services.
records.notes: Used as the terminal exchange shutdown marker.

records.title: New Zealand SFO opened investigation into Dasset
records.description: New Zealand's Serious Fraud Office opened an investigation into Digital Asset Exchange Ltd, trading as Dasset, after the company entered liquidation.
records.notes: Post-liquidation investigation marker. It is not treated as the primary death_reason.

records.title: Coinsecure founded
records.description: Company and exchange-profile references describe Coinsecure as an India-based Bitcoin exchange founded around 2014.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2014-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: 438.318 BTC stolen from Coinsecure
records.description: Coinsecure reported that 438.318 BTC had been exposed and siphoned to an address outside its control, after which the exchange became unavailable.
records.notes: Used as the terminal failure marker because later exchange references mark Coinsecure inactive / unavailable following the theft.

records.title: Coinsecure customer compensation claim deadline
records.description: Reports citing Coinsecure's compensation announcement said customers had to submit claims and supporting documents by 2018-06-30.
records.notes: Follow-up compensation deadline marker, not the terminal shutdown date.

records.title: LiteBit founded
records.description: LiteBit was founded in 2013 in Rotterdam as a European cryptocurrency broker / exchange platform.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2013-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: LiteBit announced plan to cease operations
records.description: LiteBit announced that it intended to end its activities due to market developments, increasing competition, and regulatory pressure, while offering customers a switch to Bitvavo.
records.notes: Recorded as the service wind-down announcement marker.

records.title: ACM approved Bitvavo acquisition of LiteBit customer base
records.description: The Dutch Authority for Consumers and Markets approved Bitvavo's acquisition of the LiteBit customer base.
records.notes: This event records customer-base acquisition / migration, not necessarily full corporate acquisition.

records.title: LiteBit transfer deadline and login shutdown
records.description: LiteBit's help center told users that assets and account information would be transferred to Bitvavo after 2023-08-14 and that logging in to LiteBit would no longer be available after that date.
records.notes: Used as the terminal service cutoff / migration deadline marker.

records.title: Crypto Facilities incorporated / founded
records.description: Crypto Facilities Ltd was incorporated in the United Kingdom on 2014-08-12 and is described by company-profile sources as founded in 2014.
records.notes: Uses UK incorporation date as the precise launch marker.

records.title: Kraken acquired Crypto Facilities
records.description: Kraken acquired Crypto Facilities, a London-based regulated cryptocurrency derivatives trading platform and index provider, in a nine-figure deal.
records.notes: Used as the independent-platform acquisition marker. Crypto Facilities continued under Kraken as a subsidiary / regulated venue.

records.title: BitTrade Australia founded / registered
records.description: Bit Trade was established in Australia in 2013 and later described as Australia's longest-running crypto service provider.
records.notes: Uses company registration date as the precise marker; other sources describe the service as founded in 2013.

records.title: Kraken acquired Bit Trade
records.description: Kraken acquired Bit Trade, described as Australia's longest-running crypto service provider, and brought the Bit Trade team into Kraken Australia.
records.notes: Used as the independent BitTrade Australia acquisition marker.

records.title: Urdubit launched as Pakistan's first Bitcoin exchange
records.description: Urdubit launched in October 2014 as a Pakistan-based Bitcoin exchange using BlinkTrade infrastructure.
records.notes: Uses the CoinDesk launch report date as the launch marker.

records.title: State Bank of Pakistan prohibited banks from facilitating virtual-currency transactions
records.description: The State Bank of Pakistan issued BPRD Circular No. 03 of 2018, advising banks and payment providers to refrain from processing, using, trading, holding, transferring value, promoting, investing in, or facilitating virtual currencies / tokens.
records.notes: Regulatory trigger for Urdubit's closure.

records.title: Urdubit announced shutdown after SBP circular
records.description: Urdubit announced it would close following the State Bank of Pakistan's stance on virtual currencies and urged customers to withdraw funds.
records.notes: Used as the terminal exchange shutdown marker. Some reports mention withdrawal windows continuing around 2018-04-08.

records.title: QUOINE launched QRYPTOS
records.description: QUOINE launched QRYPTOS as a fully digital cryptocurrency-only exchange and trading platform.
records.notes: Uses QUOINE / PRNewswire launch announcement date.

records.title: QUOINE announced ICO platform initially integrated with QRYPTOS before Liquid
records.description: QUOINE announced that ICO Mission Control would initially be integrated within QRYPTOS before becoming part of the forthcoming Liquid platform.
records.notes: This is a transition-context marker showing QRYPTOS functionality moving toward the Liquid platform.

records.title: QRYPTOS merged / relaunched into Liquid
records.description: QUOINE launched Liquid on 2018-09-05; Quoinex and Qryptos were put into maintenance mode shortly before launch and merged into the new Liquid platform.
records.notes: Used as the terminal independent-brand marker for QRYPTOS.

records.title: BitForex founded / launched
records.description: Exchange tracker and company materials describe BitForex as a centralized exchange founded around 2017 and active by 2018.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2017-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BitForex halted withdrawals and became inaccessible
records.description: BitForex users were unable to withdraw or access the platform after large hot-wallet outflows and a period of no communication from the exchange.
records.notes: Used as the operational failure marker, not as a final death marker.

records.title: Hong Kong SFC warned public about BitForex
records.description: Hong Kong's Securities and Futures Commission warned the public about suspected virtual asset fraud involving BitForex and placed BitForex on its Suspicious Virtual Asset Trading Platforms Alert List.
records.notes: Regulatory warning marker. SFC said BitForex was not licensed and investors were prevented from accessing accounts and withdrawing assets.

records.title: BitForex reopened for withdrawal processing only
records.description: BitForex said it would reopen for withdrawal applications after a five-month outage, while trading, deposits, and other services remained unavailable or subject to closure.
records.notes: Used to justify limited status instead of dead. This does not establish normal exchange operation.

records.title: BKEX launched
records.description: Exchange tracker and secondary sources describe BKEX as a centralized crypto exchange launched in June 2018 and registered in the British Virgin Islands.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2018-06-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BKEX suspended withdrawals amid police investigation
records.description: BKEX suspended withdrawals after stating that some platform user funds were involved in money laundering and that it was cooperating with police to collect evidence.
records.notes: Used as the operational restriction marker. This is not classified as a final death event because no primary formal closure or liquidation notice is included.

records.title: ZB.com platform lineage launched
records.description: Market-tracker references describe ZB Group's flagship ZB.com platform lineage as launching in early 2013, while other trackers describe the modern ZB exchange as established in 2017.
records.notes: Uses 2013-01-01 for the platform-lineage marker. Some sources instead describe the modern ZB exchange as established in 2017.

records.title: ZB.com suspended deposits and withdrawals
records.description: ZB.com announced that deposit and withdrawal services were suspended, citing temporary maintenance / core-application failure; market trackers continued to display this suspension notice.
records.notes: Used as the operational restriction marker. Not classified as a final death event.

records.title: Suspected ZB.com hot-wallet exploit reported
records.description: Media reports and blockchain-security monitoring described a suspected ZB.com hot-wallet exploit involving roughly USD 4.8-5 million in token outflows shortly after withdrawals were suspended.
records.notes: Recorded as suspected / reported exploit context. The exchange did not provide a clear public confirmation in the cited reports.

records.title: Allcoin launched
records.description: Market trackers and exchange-profile sources describe Allcoin as a Canada-registered centralized exchange launched around 2016.
records.notes: Date is month-level because Coin360 describes Allcoin's platform as officially released in April 2016, while other trackers store 2016 year-level establishment.

records.title: Allcoin marked inactive / closed by exchange trackers
records.description: Exchange trackers and company-profile sources later marked Allcoin inactive, closed, or untracked, with zero coins / trading pairs and no reliable market activity.
records.notes: Used as the terminal inactivity marker. This is not backed by a primary Allcoin shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown.

records.title: Neraex established
records.description: Market-tracker sources describe Neraex as a Japan-registered centralized cryptocurrency exchange established in 2017.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2017-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Neraex marked dead after apparent website takeover
records.description: Cryptowisser marked Neraex dead after reporting that the exchange website appeared to have been hacked or taken over; CoinGecko later showed no available coins, trading pairs, or reported volume.
records.notes: Used as the terminal inactivity marker. This is not backed by a primary Neraex shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown.

records.title: CoinFalcon launched
records.description: Exchange-profile sources describe CoinFalcon as a UK-based centralized cryptocurrency exchange launched in May 2017.
records.notes: Uses BitDegree's 2017-05-28 launch date; other sources give May 2017 or 2017 year-level.

records.title: CoinFalcon became inactive / no longer operational
records.description: Exchange trackers and exchange-profile sources later marked CoinFalcon inactive, untracked, or no longer operational, with no reliable active market data.
records.notes: Used as a year-level terminal inactivity marker. This is not backed by a primary CoinFalcon shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown.

records.title: AgoraDesk / LocalMonero platform era began
records.description: The official wind-down notice says the platform had operated for almost seven years before the May 2024 closure announcement, placing the platform's start around 2017.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2017-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: AgoraDesk announced wind-down
records.description: AgoraDesk / LocalMonero announced that the platform would wind down after almost seven years of operation, disabling new signups and ad postings immediately.
records.notes: Announcement / wind-down start marker.

records.title: AgoraDesk disabled new trades
records.description: The official wind-down schedule said new trades would be disabled on 2024-05-14, one week after the announcement.
records.notes: Trading-disable marker during the wind-down process.

records.title: AgoraDesk / LocalMonero wind-down completed
records.description: The official wind-down schedule said the website would be taken down after 2024-11-07 and that users should recover any remaining arbitration bond funds before that date.
records.notes: Used as the terminal shutdown marker.

records.title: BarterDEX released as Komodo atomic-swap GUI
records.description: Komodo's roadmap describes a November 2017 release of the first GUI for atomic-swaps trading, enabling trustless peer-to-peer swaps; exchange-tracker sources describe BarterDEX as launched in 2017.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2017-11-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BarterDEX retired as Komodo shifted to AtomicDEX
records.description: Komodo launched AtomicDEX beta in July 2019 as its third-generation decentralized exchange technology and described BarterDEX as the previous DEX that was officially retired.
records.notes: Used as the terminal BarterDEX lineage marker. The date is month-level from Komodo's July 2019 AtomicDEX beta launch.

records.title: AidosMarket launch era
records.description: Secondary exchange-tracker sources describe AidosMarket as launched in or around January 2018.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2018-01-01 for schema consistency. Country/origin is not fixed because sources disagree.

records.title: AidosMarket marked dead after website became inaccessible
records.description: Cryptowisser reported that AidosMarket could not be accessed on 2024-09-18 and marked the exchange as dead; other trackers show inactive, untracked, or unavailable current exchange data.
records.notes: Used as the terminal status marker. This is not backed by a primary AidosMarket shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown and confidence remains medium.

records.title: 55.com launched premium brand product token trading
records.description: A January 2019 press-release article described 55.com as launching trading of premium brand product tokens and positioning itself as an asset trading platform using blockchain technology and tokens.
records.notes: Used as the public operating marker. The platform may have existed earlier, but this is the clearest dated public-trading marker in the current evidence set.

records.title: 55 Global Markets marked dead after website became inaccessible
records.description: Cryptowisser reported on 2020-05-01 that it could not access the 55 Global Markets website and, pending contrary information, marked the exchange as dead and moved it to the Exchange Graveyard. Other trackers later showed no current market data or website-unavailable status.
records.notes: Used as the terminal status marker. This is not backed by a primary 55 Global Markets shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown and confidence remains medium.

records.title: DEEX launch era
records.description: CoinGecko and other exchange-tracker sources describe DEEX as a decentralized cryptocurrency exchange established around 2018.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2018-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: DEEX marked dead after closure report
records.description: Cryptowisser reported on 2022-12-02 that DEEX had financial issues, did not manage to get services fully operational, and closed. Other trackers later showed no active market data or an offline website.
records.notes: Used as the terminal status marker. This is not backed by a primary DEEX shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown and confidence remains medium.

records.title: BayBit.io operating era
records.description: Cryptowisser describes BayBit.io, previously Bitcoinfive.com, as a United Kingdom-based Bitcoin-only exchange service supporting wire transfer and credit card deposits.
records.notes: Launch date is year-level. Stored as 2019-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BayBit.io changed name to Vero Exchange
records.description: Cryptowisser reported that around 2020-11-01 BayBit.io changed name to Vero Exchange, and its Exchange Graveyard lists BayBit.io with the reason Rebranding.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the BayBit.io identity. Vero Exchange is not linked as successor_id because it is not yet a canonical HEI entity.

records.title: BitConnect BCC proprietary exchange operating era
records.description: The FBI stated that BitConnect Coin was first released through an ICO in November 2016 and that, for most of BCC's existence, the only place to purchase, trade, or sell BCC was through BitConnect's proprietary exchange.
records.notes: Launch date is month-level based on the FBI's November 2016 BCC release statement and exchange operating context.

records.title: BitConnect shut down lending and exchange platform
records.description: BitConnect shut down its lending and exchange platform in January 2018 after cease-and-desist actions from Texas and North Carolina securities regulators, causing the BCC market to collapse.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the proprietary BCC exchange. The shutdown was directly tied to regulatory cease-and-desist actions.

records.title: BCM / Coin Meester operating era
records.description: Kraken describes BCM, Coin Meester B.V., as founded in 2017 and as one of the Netherlands' oldest registered crypto brokers, offering buy, sell, and staking services for crypto assets.
records.notes: Launch date is year-level. Stored as 2017-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Kraken completed acquisition of BCM
records.description: Kraken announced the closing of its acquisition of Coin Meester B.V. (BCM) on 2024-09-24 as part of its European expansion strategy.
records.notes: Used as the acquisition completion marker.

records.title: BCM independent user platform transitioned after Kraken acquisition
records.description: BCM and Kraken support materials indicated that BCM clients would be migrated or directed to Kraken following the acquisition, with BCM account closure or transition communications centered on 2025-01-07.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the independent BCM Exchange user platform. death_reason remains acquisition, not unknown.

records.title: LINE launched BITBOX operating era
records.description: BITBOX launched around mid-2018 as LINE's Singapore-based global cryptocurrency exchange.
records.notes: Launch date is month-level. Stored as 2018-07-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: BITBOX replaced by BITFRONT
records.description: In February 2020, LINE closed or replaced its Singapore BITBOX exchange and launched BITFRONT as a U.S.-based global platform.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the BITBOX identity. The successor is the existing BITFRONT entity.

records.title: 247Exchange operating era
records.description: F6S describes 247Exchange as a Bangkok, Thailand company founded in 2015 and offering fiat-to-digital-currency exchange services.
records.notes: Launch date is year-level. Stored as 2015-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: 247Exchange marked inactive / MIA
records.description: Cryptowisser marked 247Exchange inactive, stating that the website no longer seemed to work and that the exchange appeared to have terminated its activities. Cryptowisser's Exchange Graveyard lists 247Exchange with a 2019-01-01 MIA marker, and CoinMarketCap lists the exchange as inactive and untracked.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker. This is not backed by a primary shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown and confidence remains medium.

records.title: 6x operating era
records.description: CoinCarp describes 6x as a Singapore digital asset trading platform officially launched on 2018-03-10. 2019 press coverage also described 6X as a Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange listed on crypto data platforms and licensed for offshore virtual currency exchange services through CEZA.
records.notes: Launch date follows CoinCarp's specific 2018-03-10 launch marker. Other 2019 sources are used as operating-era support.

records.title: 6x marked dead after website became inaccessible
records.description: Cryptowisser reported that it could not access the 6x Exchange website on 2021-03-15 and marked the exchange dead in its Exchange Graveyard. Other trackers show the exchange as untracked or without current market data.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker. This is not backed by a primary shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown and confidence remains medium.

records.title: ACDX launched public beta
records.description: ACDX announced the launch of its public beta on 2020-09-29 as a cryptocurrency derivatives exchange offering futures, perpetuals, spread trading, and structured products.
records.notes: Launch date follows ACDX's 2020-09-29 official launch announcement and supporting exchange-profile sources.

records.title: ACDX Exchange marked dead after platform became inaccessible
records.description: Cryptowisser reported on 2022-10-08 that ACDX Exchange reached only a landing page, that other pages could not be accessed, and that users reported inability to access funds. Cryptowisser moved ACDX to its Exchange Graveyard, while other trackers show the exchange as inactive or untracked.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker. This is not backed by a primary shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown and confidence remains medium.

records.title: AlfaCashier operating era began
records.description: Alfacash's own history describes the service as founded in May 2012 as an electronic currency exchange service, with Bitcoin support added in 2013 and later support for multiple cryptocurrencies.
records.notes: Launch date follows Alfacash's current history page, which states the service was founded in May 2012.

records.title: AlfaCashier rebranded to Alfacash
records.description: Alfacash says ALFAcashier rebranded to alfa.cash in 2020, and Cryptowisser's Exchange Graveyard lists AlfaCashier with a 2020-11-25 Rebranding marker.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the AlfaCashier identity. successor_id remains null because Alfacash is not yet a canonical HEI entity.

records.title: Aryana Exchange operating era
records.description: CoinMarketCap and BitDegree list Aryana as launched on 2020-07-28. Cryptowisser describes Aryana Exchange as a United Kingdom-based centralized exchange focused toward the Middle East.
records.notes: Launch date follows CoinMarketCap and BitDegree's specific 2020-07-28 marker.

records.title: Aryana Exchange marked dead after website became inaccessible
records.description: Cryptowisser reported on 2023-03-23 that it was unable to access Aryana Exchange's website and marked the exchange dead. CoinMarketCap, CoinCarp, and BitDegree show untracked, inactive, or no current market data.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker. This is not backed by a primary shutdown notice, so death_reason remains unknown and confidence remains medium.

records.title: Binance acquired JEX and launched Binance JEX identity
records.description: Binance acquired the JEX crypto-asset derivatives and spot trading platform in September 2019. JEX joined the Binance ecosystem as Binance JEX and focused on crypto derivatives products.
records.notes: Used as the Binance JEX identity start marker. JEX existed before acquisition, but HEI records this entity as Binance JEX.

records.title: JEX announced platform suspension schedule
records.description: JEX announced that, as per latest regulatory requirements, new mainland China registrations and KYC were stopped and the platform would officially cease operations on 2021-12-01.
records.notes: Announcement date follows the JEX Team date in the suspension notice.

records.title: Binance JEX ceased operations
records.description: The JEX platform officially ceased operations on 2021-12-01, while users could still log in and withdraw assets. Cryptowisser marked Binance JEX dead and moved it to its Exchange Graveyard.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the Binance JEX exchange entity.

records.title: Bingbon operating era
records.description: BingX official support materials describe the platform's development since 2018, and third-party exchange profiles describe BingX as born from a rebranding of Bingbon.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2018-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Bingbon rebranded to BingX
records.description: BingX official support materials announced the new BingX brand identity in November 2021, while Cryptowisser states that Bingbon rebranded into BingX and moved Bingbon to its inactive/graveyard classification.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the Bingbon identity. Cryptowisser mentions an effective rebrand date of 2021-11-08; HEI uses 2021-11-18 because BingX official support and major press coverage identify that as the public rebrand date.

records.title: Anyswap operating era began
records.description: Multichain's official rebrand announcement states that Anyswap was founded in July 2020 and was originally positioned as a cross-chain DEX.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2020-07-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Anyswap rebranded to Multichain
records.description: Multichain officially announced on 2021-12-16 that Anyswap would be known as Multichain. The announcement says the old anyswap.exchange site would continue to provide services but would no longer be updated, while multichain.org became the live official website.
records.notes: Used as the terminal marker for the Anyswap identity. Multichain is not yet canonical in HEI, so successor_id remains null.

records.title: Thodex operating era began
records.description: Thodex, operated by Koineks Teknoloji and founded by Faruk Fatih Özer, operated as a Turkey-based cryptocurrency exchange from around 2017.
records.notes: Date is year-level. Stored as 2017-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Thodex users lost access during platform collapse
records.description: In April 2021, Thodex users could no longer access accounts after a maintenance / temporary closure notice, while founder Faruk Fatih Özer had left Turkey. The event became the terminal operating collapse of the exchange.
records.notes: Used as the practical shutdown marker. Sources describe the freeze, user access loss, and founder flight around 2021-04-20 to 2021-04-22.

records.title: Thodex founder convicted in fraud case
records.description: A Turkish court later convicted Thodex founder Faruk Fatih Özer and imposed a very long prison sentence in September 2023, confirming the fraud-centered nature of the exchange collapse.
records.notes: Legal outcome supports scam_rug death_reason but is not the exchange's operational death date.

records.title: Txbit operating era began
records.description: Txbit operated as a cryptocurrency exchange under the txbit.io domain before its 2023 shutdown announcement.
records.notes: Date is approximate/year-level and stored as 2019-01-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: Txbit announced shutdown
records.description: Txbit announced that it would close the exchange and instructed users to withdraw funds before the final shutdown date.
records.notes: Used as the operator-announced wind-down marker.

records.title: Txbit shutdown took effect
records.description: Txbit's announced final shutdown date passed, ending the exchange's operating identity.
records.notes: Used as the terminal status marker.

records.title: CoinFLEX operating era began
records.description: CoinFLEX operated as a centralized cryptocurrency exchange before its 2022 liquidity crisis and later OPNX-linked transition.
records.notes: Date is approximate/year-level and should be refined if stronger launch evidence is later added.

records.title: CoinFLEX halted withdrawals
records.description: CoinFLEX suspended customer withdrawals during a liquidity dispute involving a large counterparty later identified by CoinFLEX leadership as Roger Ver.
records.notes: Used as the critical operating failure marker.

records.title: CoinFLEX became linked to OPNX successor path
records.description: Open Exchange / OPNX emerged as a new exchange venue connected to CoinFLEX leadership and creditor recovery plans. HEI links CoinFLEX to the existing OPNX successor record.
records.notes: Used as the supersession marker. This is not treated as a clean corporate rename.

records.title: QuadrigaCX received creditor protection after founder death and missing-funds crisis
records.description: QuadrigaCX sought and received creditor protection in Nova Scotia after founder Gerald Cotten's death and the reported inability to access large amounts of customer cryptocurrency.
records.notes: This marks the practical end of the original exchange service timeline.

records.title: QuadrigaCX moved into bankruptcy proceedings
records.description: QuadrigaCX moved from creditor-protection proceedings into bankruptcy proceedings in April 2019 after recovery and restructuring prospects became remote.
records.notes: Bankruptcy event is recorded separately from the February 2019 creditor-protection event.

records.title: OSC staff review concluded Quadriga operated as a fraud
records.description: The Ontario Securities Commission staff review concluded that Quadriga's collapse resulted from fraud by Gerald Cotten and described the case as an old-fashioned fraud wrapped in modern technology.
records.notes: Fraud characterization is attributed to the OSC staff review and reporting sources.

records.title: BTC-e service went offline during law-enforcement action
records.description: BTC-e went offline around the July 2017 law-enforcement action. Public reference material and reporting describe the 2017 outage/seizure period as the end of the original BTC-e service.
records.notes: The original BTC-e service is treated as dead. WEX-related successor activity is not merged into the canonical BTC-e entity.

records.title: Alexander Vinnik pleaded guilty to money-laundering conspiracy
records.description: The U.S. Department of Justice announced in May 2024 that Alexander Vinnik pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit money laundering in connection with BTC-e's operation.
records.notes: Resolution event for Vinnik. This does not imply the exchange itself resumed or that all earlier allegations were adjudicated.

records.title: SEC filed civil action against Coinbase
records.description: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission filed a civil enforcement action against Coinbase in June 2023 involving exchange, broker, clearing, and staking-related claims.
records.notes: Major regulatory event; it did not mark Coinbase as closed or dead.

records.title: SEC filed to dismiss civil action against Coinbase
records.description: The SEC filed to dismiss its civil enforcement action against Coinbase in February 2025, ending the lawsuit that began in 2023.
records.notes: Resolution event recorded separately from the 2023 filing because it materially changed Coinbase's regulatory-history timeline.

records.title: CFTC ordered Bitfinex to pay civil monetary penalty
records.description: The U.S. CFTC ordered Bitfinex to pay a $75,000 civil monetary penalty over financed retail commodity transactions and registration-related findings.
records.notes: Civil regulatory order; it did not make Bitfinex dead.

records.title: Bitfinex suffered major bitcoin theft
records.description: Bitfinex suffered a major security breach in August 2016. Public reporting and DOJ materials describe roughly 119,754 bitcoin stolen from the exchange.
records.notes: The exchange later continued operating; this event records the breach, not a shutdown.

records.title: U.S. authorities seized bitcoin connected to the 2016 Bitfinex hack
records.description: The U.S. Department of Justice announced arrests and the seizure of more than $3.6 billion in bitcoin connected to the 2016 Bitfinex hack, calling it the department's largest financial seizure at the time.
records.notes: This is a recovery/law-enforcement event related to the stolen funds. It does not imply Bitfinex was the accused party in the laundering case.

records.title: BitMEX agreed to CFTC and FinCEN settlement
records.description: BitMEX agreed to pay up to $100 million to settle civil charges brought by the CFTC and FinCEN related to U.S. customer access, registration, and compliance controls.
records.notes: Civil regulatory settlement; it did not make the exchange dead.

records.title: BitMEX founders pleaded guilty to Bank Secrecy Act violations
records.description: The U.S. Attorney's Office for the Southern District of New York announced that Arthur Hayes and Benjamin Delo pleaded guilty to violating the Bank Secrecy Act by willfully failing to establish, implement, and maintain an AML program at BitMEX.
records.notes: Founder-level criminal resolution. It is recorded separately from company-level events.

records.title: BitMEX fined after company guilty plea
records.description: Reuters and WSJ reported that BitMEX was fined $100 million by a U.S. judge after the company pleaded guilty to a Bank Secrecy Act-related charge over anti-money-laundering failures.
records.notes: Company-level resolution event. It follows earlier civil settlement and founder-level pleas.

records.title: Nobitex suffered major security breach
records.description: Nobitex suffered a major security incident in June 2025. Public reporting and blockchain analysis described more than $90 million in crypto assets being transferred to inaccessible addresses in an apparently politically motivated attack.
records.notes: The attack was claimed by Predatory Sparrow. Attribution beyond the group's own claim is recorded cautiously.

records.title: United States announced sanctions against Nobitex
records.description: The U.S. Treasury announced sanctions targeting Nobitex and other Iranian digital-asset exchanges. Reuters and AP reported the action and the Treasury's stated sanctions-evasion rationale.
records.notes: This event records the sanctions designation and attributed government claims. Nobitex denied direct government connections in statements reported by Reuters.

records.title: Cobinhood launched
records.description: Cobinhood operated as a centralized cryptocurrency exchange established in 2017.
records.notes: Year-level launch date from exchange database reference.

records.title: Altsbit operated as a small centralized exchange
records.description: Altsbit operated as a small centralized cryptocurrency exchange before its 2020 security incident and shutdown.
records.notes: Year-level launch date from exchange database and public background references.

records.title: Altsbit reported a major hack
records.description: Altsbit reported that a February 2020 hack caused significant cryptocurrency losses across multiple assets.
records.notes: Security incident that led to the final shutdown decision.

records.title: Altsbit shut down after hack
records.description: Altsbit announced a final withdrawal deadline and shutdown following the earlier hack-related losses.
records.notes: Used as death_date because the exchange announced it would end operations and only allow withdrawals until this date.

records.title: WEX launched after BTC-e seizure
records.description: WEX emerged after the 2017 BTC-e enforcement action and was widely described as a successor service through which BTC-e customer accounts or balances moved to a new exchange environment.
records.notes: Date is month-level and stored as 2017-09-01 for schema consistency. The entity is separate from the canonical BTC-e record.

records.title: WEX stopped operating with customer funds unresolved
records.description: Public references describe WEX as ceasing work in late 2018 without returning customer assets, with later reporting and legal action referring to missing funds from WEX accounts.
records.notes: Approximate month-level terminal marker. Used because available public references point to late-2018 failure rather than a precise official shutdown notice.

records.title: Former WEX head arrested in Poland
records.description: Polish authorities arrested Dmitry Vasilyev, described in reporting as a former WEX head, on suspicion of fraud and money laundering tied to the exchange's operations and missing funds.
records.notes: Post-closure legal context. This supports the fraud-centered collapse classification but is not used as the operational death date.

records.title: LocalBitcoins founded as a P2P Bitcoin marketplace
records.description: LocalBitcoins was founded in Finland in 2012 and operated as a peer-to-peer marketplace for Bitcoin trading between users.
records.notes: Date is month-level. Stored as 2012-06-01 for schema consistency.

records.title: LocalBitcoins announced service discontinuation
records.description: LocalBitcoins announced a staged discontinuation schedule, starting with suspension of new signups on 2023-02-09 and a planned trading suspension on 2023-02-16.
records.notes: Used as the operator-announced shutdown marker.

records.title: Coinsetter founded
records.description: Coinsetter operated as a U.S.-based bitcoin exchange and trading platform during the early regulated-exchange period.
records.notes: Year-level launch date based on public company background references.

records.title: BITFRONT launched
records.description: BITFRONT launched in February 2020 as LINE's global cryptocurrency exchange, succeeding the earlier BITBOX branding.
records.notes: Month-level launch date from exchange database reference.

records.title: Phased shutdown announced
records.description: BITFRONT announced a phased voluntary shutdown, including suspended sign-ups, later trading suspension, and a final withdrawal deadline of 2023-03-31.
records.notes: Death date is set to the scheduled withdrawal shutdown date, not the announcement date.

records.title: Yapizon / Youbit operated in South Korea
records.description: Youbit operated as a South Korean cryptocurrency exchange and was previously known as Yapizon.
records.notes: Year-level launch date based on public background references.

records.title: Yapizon hack before rebrand
records.description: The exchange, then known as Yapizon, suffered a hack in 2017 before later rebranding to Youbit.
records.notes: Context event; not the terminal collapse.

records.title: Closed and filed for bankruptcy after second hack
records.description: Youbit announced it would close and file for bankruptcy after a second cyberattack caused another major asset loss.
records.notes: Used as death_date because the exchange announced closure and bankruptcy filing on this date.

records.title: CleverCoin founded
records.description: CleverCoin operated as a Netherlands-based bitcoin exchange and trading platform during the early European Bitcoin exchange period.
records.notes: Year-level launch date based on public company background references.

records.title: Acquired by Kraken
records.description: Kraken announced that it acquired CleverCoin, expanding its European exchange footprint.
records.notes: Used as death_date because CleverCoin ceased as an independent exchange brand through the acquisition.

records.title: The Rock Trading launched
records.description: The Rock Trading operated as an Italy-based cryptocurrency exchange commonly described as active since 2011.
records.notes: Year-level launch date based on exchange database references.

records.title: Operations suspended
records.description: The Rock Trading suspended platform operations in February 2023, with later reporting and legal references describing insolvency and liquidation proceedings.
records.notes: Used as death_date because the trading platform stopped operating.

records.title: Bitsane operated as a centralized exchange
records.description: Bitsane operated as a centralized cryptocurrency exchange and was known for supporting XRP trading pairs before its 2019 disappearance.
records.notes: Year-level launch date based on exchange database and public background references.

records.title: Users reported withdrawal problems
records.description: Users reported difficulty withdrawing funds from Bitsane before the exchange website and social accounts disappeared.
records.notes: Pre-collapse access issue based on public reporting and user-loss coverage.

records.title: Bitsane disappeared from the web
records.description: Bitsane's website and social channels went offline in June 2019, leaving customers reporting lost or inaccessible funds.
records.notes: Used as death_date because the platform became inaccessible and did not return as an operating exchange.

records.title: Bter operated as an early altcoin exchange
records.description: Bter operated as a centralized cryptocurrency exchange during the early altcoin market period.
records.notes: Year-level launch date based on public exchange background references.

records.title: Bter reported major BTC theft
records.description: Bter reported a major Bitcoin theft in February 2015, one of several incidents in the early exchange-security era.
records.notes: Security incident retained as context; not used as the terminal status reason.

records.title: Bter lineage continued under Gate.io
records.description: The Bter exchange lineage continued under the Gate.io brand, which remains tracked separately as an active exchange entity.
records.notes: Used as death_date because Bter is modeled as a predecessor brand rather than the currently active Gate.io entity.

records.title: ACX operated under Blockchain Global
records.description: ACX operated as an Australian cryptocurrency exchange platform associated with Blockchain Global, formerly Bitcoin Group.
records.notes: Year-level date based on company and platform background references.

records.title: Customer withdrawals became inaccessible
records.description: ACX collapsed abruptly in late 2019, with customers unable to withdraw funds from the platform.
records.notes: Used as death_date because this marks the practical exchange-service collapse.

records.title: Blockchain Global entered administration and liquidation process
records.description: Blockchain Global later entered voluntary administration, with liquidators and regulators examining debts, creditor claims, and conduct of former directors.
records.notes: Follow-up legal and administration event supporting insolvency classification.

records.title: Vebitcoin launched
records.description: Vebitcoin operated as a Turkey-based digital asset trading platform founded in August 2017.
records.notes: Month-level launch date based on exchange database reference.

records.title: Activities stopped and investigation began

records.title: Coincheck service began
records.description: Coincheck began providing its cryptocurrency exchange service in August 2014.
